narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 71.8% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows solid strengths, like an undergraduate GPA of 3.40—which is above the typical 3.21 average—and being a full-time student, which is the norm for most applicants. However, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 5th (below the average 5.71), Year 3 decile of 3rd (well under the 5.53 average), and LSAT score of 35 (slightly below the 36.70 average) stood out as key concerns. Your upper-middle family income bracket aligned somewhat with averages, and as a white female applicant, your demographics are common in the dataset, with white students making up about 84% and females around 44%.

The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 1 decile ranking, which pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure more than any other factor. Right behind that was your Year 3 decile, also contributing significantly in the same direction by signaling weaker academic performance relative to peers. Your family income level and LSAT score followed closely, both nudging the outlook toward failure compared to stronger typical profiles. Your undergraduate GPA and racial background had moderate influence in the same concerning direction, though less pivotal. Being female was the one factor that worked in your favor, slightly pulling the prediction toward passing, unlike some patterns in the data.

Overall, these academic metrics—especially your deciles and LSAT—ranked as the top influencers, outweighing demographics or study plans in shaping this high-risk assessment. Compared to students who typically pass, your grades placed you in lower deciles, which the model sees as the biggest red flags for bar success. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your academic standing—perhaps through targeted tutoring or retaking courses to climb those school deciles—or preparing more rigorously for the LSAT if possible, as these are areas within your control. Your strong UGPA is a great foundation to build on, so highlighting future improvements in a reapplication could make a real difference. Keep pushing forward; many students turn things around with focused effort.
